coredata.dc:title: Luminescent Tb-doped Ca-deficient hydroxyapatite/agar for selective adsorption and detection of UO2 2+ ion
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Hongjuan
Wang, Xianli
Xiong, Weijie
Mao, Yuanbing
coredata.dc:description: Uranium is a hazardous radioactive pollutant commonly produced from nuclear energy development and nuclear accidents. It has posed a potentially serious threat to the environment and human health. Thus, it is crucial to develop adsorptive sensors for adsorbing and detecting uranium in the environment. Here, we have synthesized a novel eco-friendly luminescent Tb-doped Ca-deficient hydroxyapatite utilizing agar as a template (denoted as “Tb-DEF-HAP/agar”) to sensitively and selectively adsorb and detect UO2 2+ ion. Ca2+ vacancy defects within the crystal structure of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar can be tuned by changing the (Tb+Ca)/P molar ratio of the precursors, which adjusts its luminescence performance. The Tb-DEF-HAP/agar shows fluorescence intensity 3.6 times of the Tb-doped HAP synthesized with stoichiometric (Tb+Ca)/P (denoted as “Tb-HAP/agar”). The Tb-DEF-HAP/agar adsorbs and detects UO2 2+ ion sensitively with an ultra-low limit of detection of 27.2 nM and shows high selectivity over various other tested metal ions. Moreover, we have investigated the luminescent adsorbing and sensing mechanism of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar as an adsorptive sensor for UO2 2+ ion. Our work provides new and valuable insights for the future development of excellent biodegradable rare earth (RE) doped HAP-based luminescent adsorptive sensors by tailoring their chemical composition such as (RE+Ca)/P molar ratios.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescence
Ca-deficient hydroxyapatite
Adsorption
Detection
UO2 2+ ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Adsorption and detection of UO2 2+ ions in aqueous solutions
Selective and sensitive adsorbing and sensing of UO2 2+ ion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence intensity of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sensor is enhanced from the corresponding Tb-HAP/agar sensor by changing the (Tb+Ca)/P molar ratio of the used precursors from 1.67 to 1.38. Synthetic route of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar's adsorptive detection of UO2 2+ ion in aqueous solution.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar, Tb-HAP/agar and agar samples. (b) TGA plots of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar, Tb-DEF-HAP agar-free, and agar samples.
EDS elemental mapping images of (a) Ca, (b) P, (c) C, (d) O and (e) Tb, and (f) EDS spectrum of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample.

(a) XPS survey scan of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample with the corresponding detailed scans of Tb 3d as the inset. (b) XRD pattern of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample along the standard pattern of HAP (PDF#72-1243).
Proposed formation mechanism of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample.
(a) Emission spectra (λex = 377 nm) and (b) photoluminescence lifetime curves (λ ex = 377 nm and λ em = 543 nm) of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar and the Tb-HAP/agar samples.

(a) PL spectra and (b) PL intensity of the 5D4 → 7F5 transition (543 nm) of Tb3+ from the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar (2.5 mL) with UO2 2+ ion (100 µL, 0.001 M) and the other tested metal ions (100 µL, 0.01 M). (c) The concentration effect of UO2 2+ on the PL spectra of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar (λex = 377 nm) and (d) the corresponding fitting curve of the PL intensity of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar at 543 nm with UO2 2+ concentration.

(a) XPS spectra of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample before and after treated with UO2 2+ accompanying with the inset showing the detailed XPS spectrum of U 4f from the UO2 2+ treated Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample. (b) PL spectrum of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample and UV-vis spectrum of UO2 2+ ion-containing aqueous solution. (c) XRD pattern of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample before and after treated with UO2 2+. (d) PL lifetime spectra of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample measured in the absence and presence of the UO2 2+ (λ ex = 377 nm and λ em = 543 nm).
The quenching mechanisms of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar as a luminescent adsorptive sensor for sensitively and selectively adsorbing and detecting UO2 2+ ion.

head.ce:title: Luminescent Tb-doped Ca-deficient hydroxyapatite/agar for selective adsorption and detection of UO2 2+ ion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Illinois Institute of Technology, 3101 South Dearborn Street, 60616-2852, Chicago, IL 60616, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, Illinois Institute of Technology, 3101 South Dearborn Street, 60616-2852, Chicago, IL 60616, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nuclear Science and Technology, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Nuclear Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Nuclear Science and Technology, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Uranium is a hazardous radioactive pollutant commonly produced from nuclear energy development and nuclear accidents. It has posed a potentially serious threat to the environment and human health. Thus, it is crucial to develop adsorptive sensors for adsorbing and detecting uranium in the environment. Here, we have synthesized a novel eco-friendly luminescent Tb-doped Ca-deficient hydroxyapatite utilizing agar as a template (denoted as “Tb-DEF-HAP/agar”) to sensitively and selectively adsorb and detect UO2 2+ ion. Ca2+ vacancy defects within the crystal structure of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar can be tuned by changing the (Tb+Ca)/P molar ratio of the precursors, which adjusts its luminescence performance. The Tb-DEF-HAP/agar shows fluorescence intensity 3.6 times of the Tb-doped HAP synthesized with stoichiometric (Tb+Ca)/P (denoted as “Tb-HAP/agar”). The Tb-DEF-HAP/agar adsorbs and detects UO2 2+ ion sensitively with an ultra-low limit of detection of 27.2 nM and shows high selectivity over various other tested metal ions. Moreover, we have investigated the luminescent adsorbing and sensing mechanism of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar as an adsorptive sensor for UO2 2+ ion. Our work provides new and valuable insights for the future development of excellent biodegradable rare earth (RE) doped HAP-based luminescent adsorptive sensors by tailoring their chemical composition such as (RE+Ca)/P molar ratios.

ce:section.ce:para: Uranium is a hazardous substance with chemical toxicity and radioactivity, which may cause serious DNA damage, kidney failure, and destruction of biomolecules once digested or inhaled [1–3]. However, as one of the most essential raw materials, uranium is constantly consumed in the nuclear industry. With the increasing utilization of nuclear energy and development of its related technology, a larger amount of radioactive wastewater containing uranium is produced [4–9]. Thus, it has posed a severe threat to the ecological environment and human health [10–14]. Uranium mainly exists as a uranyl ion under ambient conditions. Most uranyl complexes migrate quickly in aqueous media due to their high solubility in water [15–17], easily leading to spreadable pollution. Therefore, there is a critical demand for the adsorption and detection of uranyl ions in the polluted water. An adsorptive sensor with a desirable sensitivity, selectivity, and a rapid response to uranyl ions is imperative to be developed [18,19].

Luminescent adsorptive detection has become an excellent analytical method for metal ions such as UO2 2+ owing to its high sensitivity, selectivity, real-time, speediness, low cost, and convenience [20–26]. So far, luminescent probes such as metal-organic frameworks (MOF) [2,27–30], quantum dots [4,31–34], oxygen-containing ligands [35,36], rare earth (RE)-doping nanofibers [15], and dye molecules [37,38] with outstanding performance have been developed. However, the preparation of the MOF-based sensors is commonly complicated and costly. Most dye molecules-based sensors are toxic. And the sensitivity of some sensors is still insufficient for detecting uranium in ultralow pollution areas (Table S1). Therefore, developing environment-friendly, economical and highly efficient and adsorptive luminescent sensors is still needed.

To our best knowledge, RE-doped hydroxyapatite (HAP)-based materials have not been reported as luminescent adsorptive probes for UO2 2+ adsorption and detection despite the advantages of HAP including non-toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and eco-friendliness [39,40]. In this work, we synthesized Tb-doped HAP (Tb-HAP/agar) via a facile and green biomimetic process utilizing agar as a template with the (Tb+Ca)/P molar ratio of 1.67. The biomimetic process is cost-effective without any addition of poisonous reagents and requirements of any harsh reaction conditions (such as strong alkalis and acids, high temperature, and high pressure). In addition, novel Tb-doped Ca-deficient HAP (Tb-DEF-HAP/agar) can be obtained by changing the (Tb+Ca)/P molar ratio from 1.67 to 1.38 of the stoichiometric Tb-HAP/agar form, which causes Ca2+ vacancies within the crystal structure of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar. Interestingly, the defects of Ca2+ vacancies could improve the optical performance and tune the luminescent behaviors of the obtained Tb-HAP/agar samples [41–43]. Excitingly, the fluorescence intensity of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample is enhanced by 3.6 times from the corresponding Tb-HAP/agar sample by changing the (Tb+Ca)/P molar ratio of the used precursors from 1.67 to 1.38. In one of our recent work, we developed Tb3+-doped fluorapatite/agar luminescent sensor for detecting UO2 2+, Cu2+, and Cr3+ ions [44]. In that study, sodium fluoride was added to replace the hydroxyl (–OH) group of HAP by the fluoride ion (F−) to form calcium fluorapatite, which improved the luminescence properties of the Tb3+-doped fluorapatite/agar luminescent sensor. In the current study, we improved the luminescence performance of the Tb-HAP/agar sample by changing the (Tb+Ca)/P ratio without adding any other chemical substance. This new synthetic process is more environmentally friendly and saves raw materials.

Furthermore, the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sensor could selectively adsorb and detect UO2 2+ ion in aqueous solution with high sensitivity based on luminescence turn-off effects (Scheme 1 ). We also discussed its luminescence turn-off mechanism in detail through FT-IR, XRD, XPS, emission spectra and optical excitation, UV-Vis adsorption spectra, along with the measurements of luminescence lifetime. The adsorptive detection mechanisms of UO2 2+ ion involve the structural transformation of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar, the competition for absorbing excitation spectra, and electron transfer from the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar to UO2 2+ ions.

(UO2)(NO3)2 was received from United Nuclear Corporation. Tb(NO3)3·5H2O, NaNO3, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, NaOH and agar were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Gd(NO3)3·6H2O were obtained from Acros Organics. Ca(NO3)2·4H2O were received from Fisher Scientific. Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, KNO3, La(NO3)3·6H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Sr(NO3)2, and absolute ethyl alcohol were purchased from Alfa Aesar. NH4H2PO4 were purchased from Fluka. All chemicals are of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The Tb-DEF-HAP/agar was synthesized with the molar ratio of (Tb+Ca)/P = 1.38 of the used precursors via a biomimetic synthesis route described in our previous work after some modifications [44]. Specifically, Tb(NO3)3·5H2O (0.134 g) and Ca(NO3)2·4H2O (0.744 g) were dissolved in 25 mL deionized water to obtain Solution A. Separately, 0.16 g agar used as a template in this synthetic route was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water by heating to obtain Solution B. Meanwhile, 0.288 g NH4H2PO4 was dissolved in 25 mL deionized water to acquire Solution C while its pH was adjusted to 10 using 1 M NaOH(aq). Consequently, the Solution A was first introduced into the Solution B under vigorous stirring, and then the Solution C was added into the mixture of the Solution A and the Solution B. Afterwards, the mixed solution was stirred further for 4 h after adjusting its pH to 10. Lastly, the obtained sample was acquired by centrifugation and washed by using deionized water and ethanol followed by drying in an oven at 70 ℃ for about 20 h. For comparison, the Tb-HAP/agar with a stoichiometric amount of Ca2+ were prepared through the same biomimetic method with the molar ratio of (Tb+Ca)/P = 1.67 by increasing the used amount of Ca(NO3)2·4H2O to 0.912 g. Tb-DEF-HAP agar-free sample was also synthesized with no addition of agar through the same biomimetic process.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Perkin Elmer Spectrum Two spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were recorded with an X-ray Photoelectron spectrometer (PHI model 5600) equipped with an Al Kα monochromatic X-ray source. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was acquired using a TGA apparatus (Mettler Toledo). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were conducted using a Bruker D2 Phaser diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra were obtained from a UV-vis spectrophotometer (Beckman Coulter DU 800). The morphologic characterization and elements analysis was conducted on a scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an energy dispersion X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (Phenom pro). Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were carried out using a FLS1000 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Inc.) equipped with a Xe lamp as the excitation source. The data of luminescence lifetime were collected on the same FLS1000 spectrofluorometer containing a pulsed Xe source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and detection of UO2 2+ ions in aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para: For selectivity adsorption and detection experiments, UO2 2+ ion (100 µL, 0.001 M) and the other tested metal ions (100 µL, 0.01 M) were added into quartz cuvettes containing 2.5 mL of 0.1 mg/mL emulsions of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample. The other metal ions for the selectivity test include alkali metal ions (Na+ and K+), alkaline-earth metal ions (Ca2+ and Sr2+), lanthanides ions (La3+, Ce3+, and Gd3+), transition-metal ions (Zn2+) and other main group metal ions (Al3+). These metal ions commonly co-exist with UO2 2+ ions in water. For sensitivity experiments of UO2 2+ ion, emulsions of the as-prepared Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample (2.5 mL, 0.1 mg/mL) were mixed with different concentrations of UO2 2+ ions. These mixtures in the cuvettes were shaken for 10 min to ensure a thorough reaction between the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample and UO2 2+ ions. Adsorption and detection of UO2 2+ ions in aqueous solutions were achieved from PL measurements using the FLS 1000 spectrofluorometer.

FT-IR spectrum of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar ( Fig. 1a) demonstrates a broadband centered around 3284 cm−1 and a peak at 1634 cm−1 corresponding to the characteristic vibration of the O–H group and bending vibrational mode of H−O−H of absorbed H2O, respectively[44,45]. Moreover, the vibrational modes at 1025 cm−1, 560 cm−1 and 600 cm−1 are associated with PO4 3− group[46–48]. The band at 1038 cm−1 was ascribed to vibrational mode of C–O group from agar (Fig. S1), which was overlapped with the band of -PO4 3− group on Tb-DEF-HAP/agar [49]. The peaks at around 1417 cm−1 and 873 cm−1 are assigned to the vibrational modes of carbonate group (CO3 2−), which reveals the presence of CO3 2− replacing PO4 3 − [45,50]. The peak of CO3 2− group is more intensified for the Tb-HAP/agar compared to the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar, which could be due to that Ca2+ vacancy defects in the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar make the replacement of PO4 3- by CO3 2− more difficult [51,52]. The weak band at 1455 cm−1 is ascribed to the scissor vibration mode of the C-H group from agar (Fig. S1). Meanwhile, FT-IR spectrum of the Tb-HAP/agar sample demonstrates all the characteristic peaks of HAP.

The presence of agar on the surface of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar was also confirmed by TGA analysis. As shown in Fig. 1b, a loss in weight of 18.3% from the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar between 25°C and 250°C is due to the loss of absorbed H2O and some agar. This weight loss from the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar is more than that of the Tb-DEF-HAP agar-free sample of 8% as also shown in Fig. 1b, which could be attributed to not only the agar but also more absorbed H2O on the agar-functionalized sample because of the hydrophilicity of agar. Furthermore, the loss in weight of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample from 250 °C to 500 °C is 21.6%, which is much higher than that of the Tb-DEF-HAP agar-free sample (3.8%). As is shown in Fig. S2, agar has a steep weight drop of 74.3% from 250 °C to 500 °C. Thus, the much higher loss in weight of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar than the Tb-DEF-HAP agar-free sample is due to the decomposition of the grafted agar, implying that agar was grafted with the Tb-DEF-HAP.

The morphology of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar composites was measured by SEM instrument. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluffy morphology and plenty of nano-sized particles are observed from the SEM image of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample. We also can see that a few nanoparticles softly agglomerated together. EDS spectrum and EDS elemental mapping images of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar indicate the existence of Tb, Ca, C, P, and O elements ( Fig. 2).

The XPS analysis confirms that the Tb, Ca, C, P, and O elements all present in the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample ( Fig. 3a). The XRD peaks for the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample with Ca-deficiency look the same with the crystal structure of HAP with a stoichiometric amount of calcium (Fig. 3b), well agreeing with the standard XRD pattern of HAP (PDF#72–1243). The corresponding Tb-HAP/agar sample also matches the standard pattern of HAP [PDF#72–1243] as shown in Fig. S4. No impurities are detected in both Tb-DEF-HAP/agar and Tb-HAP/agar samples, revealing that they have high purity with HAP structure [44].

As the aforementioned FT-IR, TGA, EDS, XPS and XRD analysis confirmed that the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample has been successfully synthesized by the facile biomimetic process, we have proposed a possible mechanism for the formation of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample as shown in Fig. 4. Agar containing numerous hydroxyl groups acts as nucleation sites to allow the construction of Tb-DEF-HAP crystal nucleus on the surface of agar. Initially, Tb3+ and Ca2+ ions formed from the dissolution of Tb(NO3)3·5H2O and Ca(NO3)2·4H2O in deionized water are coordinated with the hydroxyl groups on the agar surface. Afterward, the addition of NH4H2PO4 into the mixture solution at pH 10 triggers the formation of numerous nuclei and crystallization of the Tb-DEF-HAP on the agar surface [53,54].

Upon excitation at 377 nm, the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample showed the characteristic peaks of Tb3+ at 489, 543, 587, and 621 nm due to its 5D4 → 7F6, 5D4 → 7F5, 5D4 → 7F4, and 5D4 → 7F3 transitions, respectively [44] ( Fig. 5a). The PL emission peak with maximum luminescence intensity is located at 543 nm. Interestingly, the PL intensity of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar is 3.6 times higher than that of the Tb-HAP/agar. Such emission intensity difference is also confirmed by longer photoluminescence lifetime obtained from the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample (1262.38 µs) when compared with the Tb-HAP/agar (907.99 µs) (Fig. 5b and Table S2). The stronger PL intensity and longer luminescence lifetime of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar compared to the Tb-HAP/agar sample is caused by the decreasing (Tb+Ca)/P molar ratio of precursors from 1.67 to 1.38. This may be because that the decrease of the molar ratio of (Tb+Ca)/P could lead to the generation of Ca2+ vacancy defects, which can enhance the luminescence intensity of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar [41–43,55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive adsorbing and sensing of UO2 2+ ion
ce:section.ce:para: To gain insight into the adsorption and detection performance of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar, we explored its luminescent responses in the presence of different metal ions. As shown in Fig. 6a and b, luminescence intensity of the Tb-DEF-HAP showed an obvious quenching with the presence of UO2 2+ ion while it exhibited little change with other tested metal ions, including Gd3+, La3+, K+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Na+, Zn2+, Al3+, and Ce3+. This result demonstrates that the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar has the potential for selective adsorption and detection of UO2 2+ ion.

As seen from Fig. 6c, the luminescence intensity of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar decreases with increasing UO2 2+ concentration. The luminescence quenching efficiency can be described by the Stern–Volmer (SV) equation:
(1)
I 0
/ I =
1
+
K
S V
[ Q ]
where I 0 and I are luminescence intensities of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar without and with adding UO2 2+, respectively, KSV is the quenching constant, and [Q] is UO2 2+ ionic concentration. The plot of (I 0/I) versus UO2 2+ concentration shown in Fig. 6d exhibits a linear correlation. The quenching constant (KSV) is 6.85 × 104 M−1 for UO2 2+. Based on the detailed calculation described in Fig. S5, the calculated limit of detection (LOD) value of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar for UO2 2+ is 27.2 nM (7.34 ppb). This LOD value of UO2 2+ is below the pollution limit of UO2 2+ (30 ppb) in drinking wate stated by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) [31]. This LOD value for UO2 2+ ion from our Tb-DEF-HAP/agar is also lower than that of most known luminescent adsorptive sensors as compiled in Table S1.

To understand the mechanism of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar as a luminescent adsorptive sensor for sensitive and selective adsorption and detection of UO2 2+ ion, we have conducted further studies by FT-IR, XPS, UV-Vis adsorption spectra, XRD, emission spectra and luminescence excitation, along with the measurements of luminescence lifetime. The XPS spectra ( Fig. 7a) display the appearance of U peak after adding U-containing solution to the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample, which does not appear from the pristine Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample. This observation reveals that UO2 2+ ion is adsorbed onto the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample.

The XPS results are further confirmed by a new strong peak appearing at 913 cm−1 and the peak shifting representing –OH and –PO4 3− groups in the FT-IR spectrum of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample after adding UO2 2+ ion (Fig. S4). The FT-IR data also shows that UO2 2+ ion has bonded with the functional groups on the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample. These results reveal that UO2 2+ ion has adsorbed onto the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample which is responsible for the observed luminescence quenching sensitively and selectively for UO2 2+ ion (Fig. 6).

From the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of UO2 2+ shown in Fig. 7b, UO2 2+ can absorb photons at the excitation wavelength of 377 nm, which leads to competition of UO2 2+ ion with the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar for the excitation light. This result demonstrates that the competition for absorbing excitation light might also contribute to quenching the luminescence from the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar by UO2 2+ ion. While the re-absorption of the emission light by UO2 2+ does not occur because of no overlap existing between the emission spectrum of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar and absorption spectrum of UO2 2+.

XRD pattern (Fig. 7c) indicates that, after treated with the UO2 2+ solution, the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample has converted from an HAP-based structure (PDF#72-1243) into an autunite-based structure (PDF#72-2117). The formation of the autunite-based structure and the disappearance of the HAP-based structure indicates that UO2 2+ ion has adsorbed by the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample and eventually has been incorporated by the luminescent Tb-doped Ca-deficient HAP. Similar results were also found by Wu et al. [56] and Han et al. [57] in which UO2 2+ ion was immobilized by hollow HAP microspheres and wasted bio-HAP, respectively. Thus, one of possible reasons for our observed luminescence quenching could be the structure transformation of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar upon UO2 2+ adsorption.

To further deepen our understanding of the PL quenching mechanism of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar upon UO2 2+ adsorption, lifetime decays of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar were measured in the absence and presence of UO2 2+. The decay curves in Fig. 7d were fitted well by biexponential lifetime decay functions as indicated by the fitting parameters shown in Table S2. From Fig. 7d and Table S2, the average PL lifetime of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar in the absence of UO2 2+ is 1262.38 µs, while the average PL lifetime of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar in the presence of UO2 2+ has significantly reduced to 12.50 µs. The significantly declined luminescence lifetime displayed that energy or electron transfer processes could occur, leading to faster exciton decays and shorter luminescence lifetimes. For energy transfer process, an overlap is required between the emission spectra of donors and absorption spectra of acceptors [58]. As no overlap was observed between the absorption spectrum of UO2 2+ and the emission spectrum of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar (Fig. 7b), energy transfer processes can be excluded here. Therefore, it is believed that electron transfer must happen from the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar to UO2 2+ while little change of the luminescence lifetime was observed from the Tb-HAP/agar sample in the presence of other tested metal ions as shown in Table S3. These results demonstrated that no electron or energy transfer could happen between the other metal ions and the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample, thus there is no obvious luminescence quenching of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar sample in the presence of the other tested metal ions [44,59,60].

According to the results discussed above, the adsorptive detection mechanisms of fluorescence adsorbing and sensing UO2 2+ ion is illustrated in Fig. 8. After UO2 2+ ion was added into the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar emulsion, abundant OH− and PO4 3− groups on the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar facilitate UO2 2+ ion being adsorbed and incorporated by the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar and then causing the structure transformation of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar. Moreover, UO2 2+ acting as an electron acceptor on the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar as a luminescent adsorptive sensor could accept electrons in the excited state from the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar adsorber and sensor, thus leading to luminescence quenching. In addition, the competition for absorption of the excitation light by UO2 2+ could also contribute to quenching the luminescence of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar.

In conclusion, the successful biomimetic synthesis of the novel eco-friendly luminescent Tb-doped Ca-deficient hydroxyapatite on the agar template (Tb-DEF-HAP/agar) was proved by the analyses results of FT-IR, TGA, EDS, XPS and XRD. The luminescence intensity of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar was 3.6 times stronger than that of the Tb-HAP/agar with stoichiometric Ca by changing the (Tb+Ca)/P molar ratio of the used precursors. The Tb-DEF-HAP/agar showed high UO2 2+ ion adsorption and excellent detection sensitivity with the LOD value of 27.2 nM. It is below the contamination limit of uranium in the drinking water defined by the US EPA. The Tb-DEF-HAP/agar also exhibited excellent selectivity for UO2 2+ ion. The detection mechanisms of UO2 2+ ion involve the structural transformation of the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar, the competition for absorption of the excitation light, and electron transfer from the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar to UO2 2+ ion. These results indicate that the Tb-DEF-HAP/agar is a potentially sensitive and selective luminescence adsorptive sensor for UO2 2+ ion in water.

Hongjuan Liu: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Xianli Wang: Methodology, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Weijie Xiong: Writing – original draft. Yuanbing Mao: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the IIT start-up funds, the Research Foundation of Education Bureau of Hunan Province (Grant No. 18A233), the China Scholarship Council (Grant No. CSC201908430311), and the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (Grant No. 2021JJ30563). The authors also thank Drs. Z. Lee and J. Terry for XPS measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.materresbull.2022.111850.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improved AIE-active probe with high sensitivity for accurate uranyl ion monitoring in the wild using portable electrochemiluminescence system for environmental applications
Adv. Funct. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, J. Pan, Q. Li, Y. Zhou, S. Yang, J.-J. Xu, et al., Improved AIE-active probe with high sensitivity for accurate uranyl ion monitoring in the wild using portable electrochemiluminescence system for environmental applications, Adv. Funct. Mater.30(2020) 2000220.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An efficient uranium adsorption magnetic platform based on amidoxime-functionalized flower-like Fe3O4@TiO2 core–Shell microspheres
